content: Secondary prevention after myocardial infarction aims to prevent recurrent events, reduce mortality, and improve quality of life. Post-MI patients are at highest risk for recurrent ASCVD events (highest in first year, remains elevated lifelong). Comprehensive approach includes pharmacologic therapy, lifestyle modification, cardiac rehabilitation, and risk factor management. Guideline-directed medical therapy (GDMT) proven to reduce mortality and morbidity. Target levels: LDL <70 mg/dL (or <55 mg/dL very high risk), BP <130/80 mmHg, HbA1c <7% if diabetic, smoking cessation (complete abstinence), weight management (BMI 18.5-24.9), physical activity ≥150 min/week moderate intensity. Patient education on medication adherence, symptom recognition, emergency response critical.

Essential medications post-MI: (1) Dual antiplatelet therapy (DAPT): aspirin 81mg daily indefinitely plus P2Y12 inhibitor (clopidogrel 75mg, ticagrelor 90mg BID, or prasugrel 10mg daily) for 12 months minimum after ACS; shorter duration (3-6 months) considered if high bleeding risk; extended beyond 12 months if high ischemic risk and low bleeding risk. (2) Beta-blocker: metoprolol succinate, carvedilol, or bisoprolol for ≥3 years (indefinitely if LVEF ≤40%, HF, or ongoing ischemia); reduces mortality, reinfarction, arrhythmias. (3) ACE inhibitor (or ARB if intolerant): indicated for all post-MI, especially with LVEF ≤40%, anterior MI, diabetes, CKD; start within 24 hours if stable; reduces remodeling, mortality. (4) High-intensity statin: atorvastatin 40-80mg or rosuvastatin 20-40mg regardless of baseline LDL; add ezetimibe or PCSK9 inhibitor if LDL remains above goal. (5) Aldosterone antagonist: eplerenone or spironolactone if LVEF ≤40% with HF or diabetes (EPHESUS trial).

Cardiac rehabilitation: Class I recommendation for all post-MI patients. Supervised program includes exercise training, education, counseling, risk factor modification. Reduces mortality ~20-25%, hospital readmissions, improves functional capacity and quality of life. Underutilized (<30% eligible participate). Components: aerobic exercise (target 30-60 min most days), resistance training, flexibility exercises. Lifestyle modifications: smoking cessation (most impactful—reduces recurrent MI risk by 50%; use counseling, pharmacotherapy [varenicline, bupropion, NRT]), Mediterranean diet (PREDIMED trial showed 30% CV event reduction with Mediterranean diet plus nuts/olive oil vs low-fat diet), weight loss if overweight, stress management, depression screening/treatment (depression common post-MI, associated with worse outcomes), sleep optimization (screen for sleep apnea). Alcohol moderation (≤1 drink/day women, ≤2 men). Sexual activity can resume when able to climb 2 flights of stairs without symptoms (typically 1-2 weeks post-uncomplicated MI). Influenza and pneumococcal vaccination recommended.
